anchor: Sam Altman told Laurie Segall he 'miscalibrated' how much the public would distrust OpenAI's classified Pentagon deal -- and argued governments, not AI companies, should set national-security AI policy. Same week, he personally called Disney CEO Josh D'Amaro to break the news that Sora was dead. Disney found out less than an hour before the public announcement. The $1B Disney investment is dead. Sora peaked at 1M users, collapsed to under 500K, and was burning roughly $1M/day in compute while generating only $2.1M in total lifetime revenue.
angle: The CEO of the most valuable AI startup is publicly admitting multiple strategic miscalculations in a single week. The Pentagon admission is a posture reversal -- from 'move fast on defense' to 'government should control this.' The Sora numbers ($2.1M lifetime revenue vs $1M/day burn) are the clearest public admission yet that generative content is a money pit at current compute costs. Two confessions, same CEO, same week -- this isn't damage control, it's a pattern.
connection: While Altman cleans up two product-partnership failures, Anthropic's Chief Commercial Officer told Bloomberg that Claude Cowork has seen stronger enterprise adoption 'in the first few weeks' than Claude Code did. The enterprise AI market is moving forward while OpenAI's CEO is on an apology tour.
reaction: I use Claude Code every day. When I read the Sora numbers -- $2.1M total lifetime revenue against $1M/day in compute -- I did the math on my own API spend. I've burned maybe $8K on Claude tokens this year building four shipping products. Sora burned that in 12 minutes generating videos nobody paid for. The unit economics gap between 'AI that builds things' and 'AI that generates content' is wider than I realized. The Pentagon admission hits different too. I watched OpenAI sprint into defense contracts and thought 'they're moving fast.' Turns out even Altman thinks they moved too fast. That kind of public recalibration from a CEO at this scale doesn't happen unless the internal data was worse than the optics.
open_questions: Is the Pentagon deal actually dead or just reframed? 'Government should control this' could also mean 'we'll take the contract under government terms'
How much of the Disney timeline -- less than an hour's notice -- was strategic vs genuinely chaotic?
Does Altman's 'still talking' with Disney mean anything, or is that just CEO face-saving?
do_not_include: Claude Code source leak details -- already posted 4/01

anchor: Total pro-AI political spending for the 2026 midterms is approaching $300M. Innovation Council Action -- led by former Trump deputy CoS Taylor Budowich and championed by White House AI advisor David Sacks -- plans $100M+ with a scorecard ranking lawmakers on alignment with Trump's AI deregulation agenda. Combined with Leading the Future ($125M) and Meta's $65M state-level super PAC, the money targets battleground states Iowa, Kentucky, Maine, Michigan, and North Carolina. Same week, Sam Altman told the Mostly Human podcast that 'democratically elected institutions -- not AI companies -- should set national-security AI policy.'
angle: This is the first election cycle where AI is a top-tier campaign issue with serious money behind it. The scorecard changes incentives -- lawmakers get graded on AI policy with $300M to enforce those grades. But the tension is the real story: $300M is being spent to elect lawmakers who will deregulate AI, while one of AI's biggest CEOs publicly asks those same institutions to provide oversight. The money and the rhetoric are running in opposite directions.
connection: Sam Altman's simultaneous call for government oversight creates a direct tension with the $300M deregulation machine. One side of the industry is building a political apparatus to remove guardrails while another CEO is publicly saying guardrails should come from government.
reaction: I build with AI every day. I don't have a lobbyist. Nobody at the independent builder scale does. $300M buys a lot of policy. The scorecard is the part that stays with me -- lawmakers getting graded on 'alignment with Trump's AI agenda' means the incentive structure just changed for every tech vote in Congress. The question I keep coming back to: are these scorecards scoring for things that help builders like me, or for things that help companies at the trillion-dollar scale? Because deregulation that benefits Meta's $65M super PAC and deregulation that benefits a solo engineer shipping with Claude Code are not always the same policy.
open_questions: What specific policy positions does the Innovation Council Action scorecard actually grade on?
Is the $300M figure cumulative committed or projected including future pledges?
How many of the battleground state races will AI deregulation actually be a decisive issue vs. noise?
do_not_include: Supply chain security findings -- already posted 4/01

anchor: In a single trading session, three of the largest SaaS incumbents simultaneously proved their AI pricing pivots are working -- ServiceNow's 'assist tokens' crossed $600M+ ACV, Salesforce's 'agentic work units' hit $800M run rate, Adobe's 'generative credits' are scaling fast. All three surged 6-8%. Same week, Gartner revised 2026 worldwide software spending upward to $1.4 trillion at 14.7% growth. Each company independently invented its own consumption unit rather than standardizing. The seat is dead. The question is what replaces it.
angle: Ten days ago, the thesis was SaaS multiples collapsing and $265B in private credit at risk. The question was 'how fast can a legacy SaaS company pivot to consumption pricing?' Now we have the answer: fast enough to stage the biggest software rally since the SaaSpocalypse. The market isn't punishing SaaS anymore -- it's separating the companies that figured out AI pricing from the ones still charging per seat. Three companies, three different AI consumption units, same validation day.
connection: The fragmentation is telling -- 'assist tokens' vs 'agentic work units' vs 'generative credits' are three different mental models for what AI work is worth. This is early-market behavior. The eventual winner will be whatever unit becomes the 'CPU hour' of AI-era software -- the one enterprises standardize on.
reaction: I've been evaluating every SaaS tool in my stack against 'could an agent replace this seat.' Looks like ServiceNow, Salesforce, and Adobe asked the same question -- and answered it by making the agent the billable unit. I run 12 agents every morning on a cron job. If ServiceNow can charge per assist token, the pricing model for agent-delivered work just got validated at enterprise scale. What I didn't expect: three companies inventing three different consumption units on the same timeline. Nobody coordinated this. They all independently arrived at 'stop charging for the seat, start charging for the outcome.' That's a market signal, not a trend piece.
open_questions: Which consumption unit wins -- tokens, work units, or credits? Or does each category settle on its own standard?
How many mid-tier SaaS companies that didn't pivot are now in serious trouble?
Does the $1.4T Gartner number hold if consumption pricing cannibalizes per-seat revenue faster than AI revenue ramps?
mindpattern_context: I run 12 agents every morning on a cron job -- the consumption pricing model these companies validated is the same economic logic behind agent-delivered work at any scale
